contest.title: Codeforces Round 1092 (Unrated, Div. 1, Based on THUPC 2026 Finals)

sources.notes: Statement and constraints: run-twice communication problem where Alice orients a tree and Bob recovers s.
Luogu mirror used to cross-check run-twice format and no-hacks note.
Used to confirm the XOR labeling strategy and DFS decoding.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: Run-twice communication problem: Alice knows n and s and sees tree edges online, must orient each edge; Bob later receives the directed tree and must recover s.
annotation.constraints: 3 <= n <= 30 and 1 <= s <= 2^(n-1), so n-1 bits are enough to encode s-1.
annotation.core_idea: Assign bit i-2 of s-1 to vertex i, with vertex 1 fixed to 0. Alice orients an edge according to whether endpoint labels are equal or different relative to a fixed lower-index convention. Bob interprets each directed edge as an XOR constraint between endpoint labels and DFSes from vertex 1 to recover all vertex bits, hence s. This is a communication-format problem, not a normal batch problem, but it is useful for communication-protocol search and fits the repository's `paradigm/communication` scope.
annotation.complexity: O(n) per test case in each run.
tricks: use tree orientations as a communication channel for n-1 bits
encode s-1 as vertex parity labels
decode edge directions as XOR constraints

solution_variants.name: xor-label-communication
solution_variants.summary: Alice maps s-1 to binary labels on vertices 2..n and orients each edge by endpoint XOR; Bob treats edge orientation as a parity constraint and reconstructs all labels from root 1.

tags.evidence: The central task is to design an encoding/decoding protocol between the first and second run.
Edge orientation is interpreted as the XOR parity difference of endpoint labels.
The solution constructs an orientation rule realizing a valid code for every s and every tree.
The problem uses Codeforces run-twice interactive/communication format.
Decoding relies on the graph being a tree, so parity labels are uniquely recovered from a fixed root.
Bob performs DFS over the tree to propagate recovered parity labels.
The delivered integer s is represented by the n-1 bits of s-1.
